steps.text: Recalibrate the gas dilution system once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty ≤ 0.25%.

Report the results of the calibration, listing the date of the most recent calibration, the due date for the next calibration, calibration point, reference flow device (device ID, S:N ratio), and acceptance criteria.
Verify the gas dilution system to be non-biasing under HFC zero air and known standards at least once per calendar year for each reactive target compound.
Flow zero air through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold used and verify it is not detectable for the target compounds.

Flow a known standard within the calibration range of the analytical system for each target compound through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold to allow equalization and verify it does not bias the standard by ± 15% of the concentrations in the reference sample.
Verify the gas dilution system daily or per use (whichever is less stringent) per Section 3.2 of Method 205 using any available protocol gas and corresponding reference method.
Flush the sampling device or system with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for a minimum of 24 hours before field deployment.
Use the analytical instrumentation to analyze humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air from a known clean source at the installation.

Examine chromatograms for interferences and other chromatographic artifacts such as nontarget peak responses, large peaks or rises in the chromatogram due to undifferentiated compounds, and baseline anomalies.

If you identify exceedances of target compounds in the zero-air challenge, take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Repeat the analytical zero-air verification to ensure that you have mitigated any problems before using the analytical instrumentation.
Heat canisters by placing them in an enclosed oven during cleaning to facilitate removal of compounds.
Evacuate canisters to minimally 7 kPa absolute (28 in. Hg vacuum or 1 psia) and maintain this vacuum for at least 1 minute.
Pressurize canisters to 414 kPa absolute (≤ 30 psig) with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air and maintain this pressure for a minimum of 1 minute.
Repeat the cycle of canister evacuation and pressurization at least 5 times (or 10 to 20 times if needed to remove stubborn interferents).
Pressurize each canister from the batch with humidified HCF zero air and maintain that pressure for at least 24 hours.
Connect each canister to the analytical system and measure the concentration of each target compound according to the procedures in Section 11.
Evacuate canisters to ≤ 0.0067 kPa (≤ 0.002 in. Hg or 0.001 psia) prior to field deployment.
Follow the manufacturer’s instructions for cleaning components such as flow controllers and sampling unit parts, when necessary.
Repeat analysis.
Reprepare the MB canister and reanalyze.
Check the system for leaks.
Prepare one CB with each set of calibration standard canisters and analyze with each ICAL.
Reanalyze CB.
Reprepare CB and ICAL canister standards.
Check for preconcentrator volume measurement error.
Reanalyze primary sample and collocated duplicate.
Flag data for possible invalidation.
Perform one replicate analysis with each sampling day.
Pressurize or evacuate the canister connection to verify as leak-free.
Check the tightness of all fittings and recheck.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to an evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as you add gases.
Add stock and diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Measure the canister pressure before and after standard and diluent gases are bled into the canister, and input these pressures into the calculation of the dilution factor and final concentrations.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.
Condition preconcentrator traps when first installed to eliminate contaminants that act as interferences or chromatographic artifacts, per manufacturer recommendation.
Analyze the IBs and MBs to verify the preconcentrator system meets the method criteria after the recommended conditioning procedure is completed.

Optimize GC conditions for compound separation and sensitivity as indicated by baseline separation for the targeted compounds by establishing GC carrier gas flow rates, oven temperature program, and instrument run time based on the manufacturer’s recommendations and customize, as needed, to separate the desired target oHAPs.
Tune/optimize the MS (quadrupole, ion trap, or TOF MS) to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications.

Initiate the quantitation process using the software package of choice to provide quantitative results compound by relating the area response ratio of each target ion for each target compound to the RRF daily CCV.
Calculate an instrument dilution correction factor (IDCF) using Equation 14 in Section 12.
Determine the final concentration of each target compound in air using Equation 15 in Section 12 by multiplying the instrument-detected concentration by the CDCF and the IDCF.

preconditions.text: When the dilution system is used
Successful completion of the known-standard challenge
Prior to initial use and as part of an instrument's annual calibration
Canister background concentration for each target compound must be ≤ 20 pptv (0.02 ppbv) or undetected (blank criteria met).
If CB is not sufficiently clean such that positive bias is imparted to the calibration.
If Method precision is > 30% RPD of target VOCs in the compared samples when both measurements are ≥ fivefold MDL.
Prior to initial use, upon replacement of transfer lines, or after analysis of potentially contaminating samples.
Prior to initial use and upon replacement of transfer lines.
Prior to initial use and annually thereafter.
Prior to initial field deployment and every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples.

Prior to initial field deployment and at least every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples or damaging sample matrices that may impact the activity of the flow path surfaces.
Prior to ICAL and prior to each day’s analysis.

Before sample analysis, following failed BFB tune check (as applicable), failed IS criteria, or failed CCV criteria; annually, or when changes/maintenance to the instrument affect calibration response.
Prior to analyzing samples in an analytical sequence and at the end of a sequence, unless the sequence begins with an ICAL; recommended after every 10 sample injections.
Prior to ICAL and at the beginning of an analytical sequence.
Prior to and following the ICAL and daily following the IB/BFB and prior to the initial daily CCV/SSCV.
Each canister connected to the instrument prior to analysis.
Upon initial installation of the instrument or following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities that impact the performance of the GC-MS system.
Peak areas have been determined.
An aliquot from the sample canister was analyzed that is different from the typical analysis volume.

constraints.text: Potential physical interferents (such as leaks of the sampling system or introduction of foreign material like particulate matter, insect nests, spider webs) could impact the ability to properly time-integrate the sampling.

Failure to adhere to the cleaning and certification requirements included in this method may lead to interference issues, including incomplete deactivation of canister interior surfaces, entrained PM deposition, and changes in air sample composition over time.
The gas dilution system must produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
The equalization time for the bias verification must be used at a minimum for the development of standards.
The volume of air analyzed must be consistent with the laboratory’s typical canister sample injection volume.
The analysis must show that the concentration of any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample is < 20 pptv or undetected (whichever is more stringent) per compound of interest.
Do not use heat bands or heating jackets.
Use the temperatures specified in Table 1 of Section 17 for canister cleaning procedures.
Disassembly of instruments may void warranties or calibrations.
CB must be sufficiently clean such that little or no positive bias is imparted to the calibration.
Method precision must be ≤ 30% RPD of target VOCs in the compared samples when both measurements are ≥ fivefold MDL.
Instrument precision must be ≤ 25% RPD for target VOCs when both measurements are ≥ fivefold MDL.
< 3.4 kPa (0.5 psi) change per minute or as recommended by the manufacturer (see Section 11.4.2 of this method).
Standards prepared in canisters at ambient laboratory conditions must be stored in locations that are free of potential contaminants for up to 7 days.
For preconcentrator traps that contain multiple types of sorbent beds, the oven temperature must not exceed the lowest conditioning temperature of the sorbents contained in the trap.
The pressure transducer or gauge must meet the requirements in Section 6.5 of this method.
The target and qualifier ion peaks must be co-maximized (i.e., peak apexes within one scan of each other).
The MDL reported with the final concentration data will be corrected by multiplying the MDL by the CDCF and IDCF applied to the sample concentrations.
